refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative uptime / total period; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Verification requires instrumentation of the Network Link and Teleoperation subsystems to log availability events.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe-state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant or failover paths to achieve ≥0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path network architecture (primary + backup link) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log availability events.
DP-B: Implement single high-reliability network link with redundant hardware (dual modems, dual antennas) and error correction; continuous health monitoring.
DP-C: Implement multi-carrier network strategy (cellular + satellite + private network) with intelligent switching logic; continuous link state monitoring and failover orchestration.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement multi-carrier network strategy (cellular + satellite + private network) with intelligent switching logic; continuous link state monitoring and failover orchestration. This DP provides the highest availability margin by eliminating single points of failure at the carrier level, aligns with safety-critical requirements, and supports geographic coverage variability across worksites.

potential_sources: DP-C couples to FR-NGHE-010.3 (network infrastructure redundancy) and FR-NGHE-010.1 (link loss detection latency).
DP-C introduces dependency on external carrier availability (cellular, satellite providers), which is outside NGHE control but can be contractually managed.
DP-C couples to safety-state transition logic (FR-NGHE-010.2), requiring coordinated design with Safety Services.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable because it is explicit and manageable. The multi-carrier strategy decouples NGHE from any single network provider failure mode, improving overall system independence.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer worksites have access to at least two independent network carriers (cellular + satellite or private network).
ASSUMPTION: Switching latency between carriers can be kept below the safe-state transition timeout (typically <500 ms for heavy equipment).

RISK: Satellite link latency (>500 ms round-trip) may exceed real-time control requirements for certain operations; mitigation: restrict satellite to telemetry-only or low-bandwidth tasking during primary link loss.
RISK: Multi-carrier orchestration logic is complex; potential for bugs in failover decision-making; mitigation: extensive simulation and field testing.
RISK: Cost and power consumption of multi-carrier hardware may exceed customer budget; mitigation: offer tiered options (DP-B for cost-sensitive, DP-C for safety-critical).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly define carrier selection logic (e.g., priority order, health thresholds, switching hysteresis) and specify maximum acceptable switching latency. Add requirement for graceful degradation: if only one carrier remains available, system shall continue operation at reduced capability with explicit operator notification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the multi-carrier network orchestration logic, link health monitoring, failover decision-making, and safe-state transition triggering. It interfaces with Vehicle (for safe-state commands), Safety Services (for SafetyAlert coordination), and external Network Link providers (cellular, satellite, private). The module includes software for carrier selection, link state machine, and availability logging.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands from tele-operator; Teleoperation module must validate link quality before accepting tasking.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and link health metrics back to tele-operator; Teleoperation module packages and prioritizes telemetry based on available carrier bandwidth.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints from Safety Services; sends link-loss alerts to Safety Services to trigger safe-state transition. Bidirectional coordination ensures safety-critical link failures are handled consistently.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interfaces with physical network infrastructure (cellular modem, satellite transceiver, private network gateway). Teleoperation module monitors link state, initiates failover, and logs availability metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link orchestration, failover logic, and availability monitoring. This is the core responsibility of the module. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert coordination requires external dependency.

Safety Services could own link availability as a safety constraint, but this would scatter network orchestration logic across two modules. Link availability is primarily a communication concern, not a safety logic concern. Recommend Safety Services owns safe-state transition policy, Teleoperation owns link management.

Vehicle module should not own network orchestration; it is a consumer of teleoperation commands, not a provider of link management. High diffusion risk if Vehicle tries to manage its own network failover.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but should not directly implement link orchestration; this is a subsystem responsibility. Recommend NGHE Solution owns the integration and coordination between Teleoperation and Safety Services, but Teleoperation owns the implementation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-C (multi-carrier network orchestration) because: (1) it is the logical subsystem responsible for remote command-and-control communication, (2) it directly interfaces with Network Link providers, (3) it owns the link state machine and failover logic, (4) it minimizes diffusion by centralizing orchestration in one module, and (5) it maintains clear separation of concerns from Safety Services (which owns safe-state policy, not link management).
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module becomes too large (orchestration + failover + logging + carrier management), it may become difficult to test and maintain. MITIGATION: Decompose into sub-modules (LinkOrchestrator, FailoverController, AvailabilityLogger) within Teleoperation boundary.

RISK: Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface could create circular dependencies if not carefully managed. MITIGATION: Define SafetyAlert as a one-way notification (Teleoperation -> Safety Services) with explicit contract.

RISK: External Network Link providers (cellular, satellite) are outside NGHE control; if a carrier becomes unavailable, Teleoperation cannot compensate. MITIGATION: Contractual SLAs with carriers; customer responsibility to ensure multi-carrier availability at worksite.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the Teleoperation module's internal architecture: separate LinkOrchestrator (owns carrier selection and failover logic), FailoverController (owns state machine and safe-state triggering), and AvailabilityLogger (owns metrics collection). This internal decomposition improves testability and maintainability while keeping the module boundary clear.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (multi-carrier network orchestration) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP provides a clear solution path: eliminate single points of failure by distributing across multiple carriers, monitor link health continuously, and failover automatically. The DP is feasible, measurable, and aligned with safety-critical requirements. No decoupling issues detected.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a unidirectional notification contract with explicit message schema.
DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): Network Link interface couples Teleoperation to external carriers. Mitigation: abstract carrier interface to allow pluggable implementations (cellular, satellite, private).

DIFFUSION RISK (MEDIUM): Failover logic may require coordination with Vehicle module (e.g., safe-state commands). Mitigation: Teleoperation owns failover decision, Vehicle owns safe-state execution; clear separation via Tasking interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the optimal container for DP-C. Containment score 0.95 indicates minimal diffusion. The module owns the core orchestration logic, interfaces with Network Link providers, and coordinates with Safety Services via SafetyAlert. No alternative module provides better containment without introducing significant diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-010 is feasible via continuous monitoring of link state transitions during the evaluation period. Teleoperation module must log all link up/down events with timestamps. Availability is calculated as cumulative uptime / total period. Verification requires instrumentation of Network Link interface and AvailabilityLogger sub-module.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≥0.999 link availability. Success criteria: (1) multi-carrier orchestration logic implemented and tested, (2) failover latency <500 ms, (3) availability metrics logged and reported, (4) field evaluation demonstrates ≥0.999 availability over agreed period. Ownership is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Refine DP-C to specify carrier priority order, health check intervals, and switching hysteresis to prevent oscillation between carriers.
REVISION 2: Define SafetyAlert interface contract explicitly (message schema, timing, error handling) to reduce coupling risk between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
REVISION 3: Decompose Teleoperation module into sub-modules (LinkOrchestrator, FailoverController, AvailabilityLogger) to improve testability and maintainability.
REVISION 4: Add acceptance test plan for link availability verification, including simulation scenarios (carrier loss, latency spikes, failover timing) and field evaluation protocol.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, safe-state transition, network redundancy, availability logging). DP-C is sound and well-aligned with the refined FR. Teleoperation is the optimal IM boundary with minimal diffusion. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to define detailed design parameters (e.g., detection latency thresholds, safe-state transition logic, logging schema). Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate these sub-FRs to specific design parameters and implementation modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds and trigger safe-state transition.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall execute safe-state transition (e.g., stop vehicle, engage brakes) upon link loss, coordinated with Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant or failover paths (multi-carrier) to achieve ≥0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics (uptime, downtime, failover events) for post-evaluation analysis and reporting.

assumptions: Customer worksites have access to at least two independent network carriers (cellular + satellite or private network).
Switching latency between carriers can be kept below the safe-state transition timeout (typically <500 ms).
Safety Services module owns safe-state transition policy; Teleoperation owns link management and failover orchestration.
Network Link interface abstracts carrier-specific details (cellular modem, satellite transceiver, private gateway).
Evaluation period is defined by customer (e.g., 30 days of continuous operation).
open_risks: RISK: Satellite link latency (>500 ms round-trip) may exceed real-time control requirements; mitigation strategy needed.
RISK: Multi-carrier orchestration logic is complex; potential for bugs in failover decision-making; extensive testing required.
RISK: Cost and power consumption of multi-carrier hardware may exceed customer budget; tiered options needed.
RISK: External carrier availability is outside NGHE control; contractual SLAs required.
RISK: SafetyAlert coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services could create circular dependencies; interface contract must be explicit.
evidence_needed: Carrier availability data for target worksites (cellular coverage, satellite availability, private network options).
Failover latency measurements from prototype or simulation (target: <500 ms).
Cost and power consumption estimates for multi-carrier hardware.
SafetyAlert interface specification and coordination protocol with Safety Services.
Field evaluation plan and acceptance test protocol for link availability verification.
Simulation scenarios for carrier loss, latency spikes, and failover timing.
